blocks.text: HashMap of value→index; for each x check if target−x was seen. O(n) time, O(n) space.
At each index either extend the running sum or restart from the current element; track the max. O(n) time, O(1) space.

questions.qText: Longest substring without repeating characters (sliding window).

blocks.text: Expand a window with the right pointer; when a duplicate appears, move the left pointer past its last occurrence. Track max length with a lastIndex map. O(n).

questions.qText: Explain the two-pointer and sliding-window patterns and when to use them.

blocks.text: Two pointers: sorted arrays or comparing from both ends (pair sums, palindrome, container with most water). Sliding window: contiguous subarray/substring with a constraint (max sum of size k, longest with condition) — grow/shrink the window instead of re-scanning. Both turn O(n²) brute force into O(n).
Re-point each node's next to the previous node while walking forward. O(n) time, O(1) space.

lines: while(h!=null){ ListNode n=h.next; h.next=p; p=h; h=n; }

questions.qText: Detect a cycle in a linked list (Floyd's).

blocks.text: Slow pointer moves 1, fast moves 2; if they meet there's a cycle. To find the start, reset one pointer to head and advance both by 1 until they meet. O(n) time, O(1) space.
Push openers on a stack; on a closer pop and check the match; valid iff the stack is empty at the end. O(n).

questions.qText: Daily temperatures / next greater element (monotonic stack).

blocks.text: Keep a stack of indices with decreasing values; when the current value is greater, pop and record the distance/next-greater. Each index is pushed/popped once → O(n). The monotonic-stack pattern solves 'next greater/smaller' families.

questions.qText: Binary tree traversals — the four you must know.

blocks.text: Preorder (root, left, right), inorder (left, root, right — sorted for a BST), postorder (left, right, root), and level-order (BFS with a queue). Recursion for DFS; a queue for BFS. All O(n).
Recurse carrying a (min, max) range; each node must lie strictly within it, tightening the range as you descend. An inorder traversal being strictly increasing is an equivalent check. O(n).
Scan the grid; on each unvisited '1' increment the count and flood-fill (DFS/BFS) its connected land to '0'. O(rows×cols). Classic connected-components problem.

questions.qText: Explain topological sort and where it's used.

blocks.text: Ordering of a DAG so every edge u→v has u before v. Kahn's algorithm: repeatedly remove nodes with in-degree 0. Used for build/task scheduling, course prerequisites, dependency resolution. If a cycle exists, no valid ordering (detects cycles). O(V+E).

questions.qText: BFS vs DFS — when to use which?

blocks.text: BFS explores level by level (queue) — best for shortest path in unweighted graphs and level-order needs; space can be large (whole frontier). DFS goes deep (stack/recursion) — best for path existence, connected components, topological sort, backtracking; lower memory but not shortest-path.
Ways(n) = ways(n−1) + ways(n−2) — Fibonacci. Use O(1) space with two rolling variables instead of an array.
dp[a] = min coins to make amount a; dp[a] = min over coins c of dp[a−c]+1. O(amount × coins).

lines: int[] dp=new int[amt+1]; Arrays.fill(dp, amt+1); dp[0]=0;
for(int a=1;a<=amt;a++) for(int c:coins) if(c<=a) dp[a]=Math.min(dp[a],dp[a-c]+1);

blocks.text: Both cache subproblem results. Memoization is top-down recursion with a cache (only computes needed states, risk of deep recursion). Tabulation is bottom-up iteration filling a table (no recursion overhead, sometimes space-optimizable). Same time complexity; pick by readability and stack constraints.

Backtracking = choose → recurse → un-choose. Build a partial solution, recurse deeper, then undo the choice to explore alternatives. Powers subsets, permutations, combinations, N-Queens, and word search. Complexity is exponential (that's the nature of enumerating all solutions).

A process is an independent program with its own memory and resources; a thread is a unit of execution inside a process sharing its memory but with its own stack. Processes are isolated (one crash doesn't kill others) but heavier; threads are lightweight but a bad thread can corrupt the process.

TCP is connection-oriented and reliable (handshake, ordering, retransmission, flow control) — HTTP, email. UDP is connectionless, unordered, no retransmission — faster and lighter, for streaming, gaming, DNS, VoIP where speed beats reliability.

questions.qText: What happens when you type a URL and press Enter?

blocks.text: DNS resolves the domain to an IP; a TCP connection opens (3-way handshake) on 443; TLS handshake establishes encryption; the browser sends an HTTP GET; the server (via load balancers/app servers/DB) returns HTML; the browser parses it, fetches CSS/JS/images, builds the DOM, and renders.

questions.qText: Explain Big-O and analyze a nested loop.

blocks.text: Big-O describes how runtime/space grows with input size, ignoring constants. A single loop over n is O(n); a loop inside a loop over n is O(n²); halving each step (binary search) is O(log n); a loop that does a log-n operation each iteration is O(n log n). Always state best/average/worst where they differ (e.g., quicksort).

Atomicity (all-or-nothing), Consistency (constraints hold), Isolation (concurrent txns don't corrupt each other), Durability (committed data survives crashes). Money transfer is the canonical example.

questions.qText: How do indexes work and when do they hurt?

blocks.text: A B-tree index gives O(log n) lookups, range scans, and sorted reads; composite indexes follow the leftmost-prefix rule. They cost write time + storage, help little on low-cardinality columns, and functions on the column (WHERE YEAR(col)) prevent index use. Verify with EXPLAIN.

questions.qText: Explain the JOIN types; find employees with no orders.

blocks.text: INNER returns matches only; LEFT returns all left rows + NULLs for missing right; RIGHT the reverse; FULL both. To find employees with no orders, LEFT JOIN and filter where the right key IS NULL.

lines: LEFT JOIN orders o ON o.emp_id = e.id

questions.qText: Explain transaction isolation levels and their anomalies.

blocks.text: READ UNCOMMITTED (dirty reads) → READ COMMITTED (no dirty reads, but non-repeatable reads) → REPEATABLE READ (stable rows, phantoms possible; MySQL default) → SERIALIZABLE (full isolation, lowest concurrency). Pick the lowest level that preserves your invariants, adding locking/versioning where money is involved.

questions.qText: SQL vs NoSQL — how do you choose?

blocks.text: Relational for transactions, joins, structured data, strong consistency (payments, orders). Document (MongoDB) for flexible schema and aggregate reads (catalogs, profiles). Key-value (Redis) for caching/sessions. Default to relational; adopt NoSQL for a specific access pattern, not fashion.

SRP (one reason to change), OCP (open for extension, closed for modification), LSP (subtypes substitutable for base types), ISP (small focused interfaces), DIP (depend on abstractions, not concretions). Together they yield loosely-coupled, testable, extensible code.

questions.qText: Which design patterns do you actually use?

blocks.text: Creational: Singleton (one instance), Factory/Builder (construction). Structural: Adapter, Decorator, Facade, Proxy. Behavioral: Strategy (swap algorithms), Observer (event notification), Template Method. Name the problem each solves rather than reciting definitions.

Entities: ParkingLot → Levels → Spots (typed: small/medium/large, EV). Vehicle hierarchy (Car/Bike/Truck). A ParkingStrategy assigns the nearest fitting spot (Strategy pattern); Ticket tracks entry/exit; a Payment component computes fees. Discuss concurrency (two cars, one spot → atomic allocation) and extensibility (new vehicle/spot types via OCP).

HashMap for O(1) lookup + a doubly-linked list ordered by recency; on access move the node to the front, evict from the tail when full. In Java, LinkedHashMap(accessOrder=true) with removeEldestEntry does it in a few lines.

POST long→short, GET short→301. Keys: base62 of a unique/snowflake id (or random + collision check). Read-heavy → Redis cache + DB read replicas. Estimate QPS/storage; discuss 301 vs 302 (caching vs analytics) and custom aliases/expiry.

Clients hold a persistent WebSocket to stateful chat servers behind a session-aware LB; a pub/sub layer (Redis/Kafka) delivers messages across servers; messages persist in a wide-column store (Cassandra) partitioned by chatId; presence in Redis; media in S3; push via FCM/APNs for offline users.

questions.qText: Explain CAP theorem and consistent hashing.

blocks.text: CAP: under a network partition you choose Consistency or Availability (partitions are unavoidable). Consistent hashing places nodes and keys on a ring so adding/removing a node remaps only ~1/N keys (not all) — used by distributed caches, DynamoDB, Cassandra; virtual nodes even out distribution.

questions.qText: How do you scale reads and writes on a database?

blocks.text: Reads: caching (Redis), read replicas, denormalized read models/CQRS, and covering indexes. Writes: sharding by a good key, batching, write-optimized stores, and async processing via queues. Always measure the real bottleneck before scaling.

questions.qText: A production API's latency spiked after a deploy. Walk me through it.

blocks.text: Roll back if impact is high, then diff the deploy (code/config/deps). Use traces to find where time goes (DB, external call, GC). Common causes: an introduced N+1, missing index on a new query, connection-pool shrink, GC pauses, or a retry storm. Present a method, not guesses.

questions.qText: Users report intermittent wrong data. How do you approach it?

blocks.text: Reproduce with specific inputs, add correlation-id logging to follow one request across services, check for race conditions (shared mutable state, missing locks/idempotency), cache staleness, and read-after-write on replicas. Form a hypothesis, verify with data, fix, then add a regression test.

questions.qText: How do you take a feature from dev to production safely?

blocks.text: Feature flag → code review → tests (unit + integration) → staging with prod-like data → canary/percentage rollout while watching error rate/latency/business metrics → full rollout → flag cleanup, with a rollback path throughout.

60 seconds: who you are (years, domain), the impact you've had (a measurable win), your strengths relevant to this role, and why you're excited about THIS team. End pointed at the job, not a life story.

questions.qText: Tell me about your most challenging project.

blocks.text: STAR: the hard problem, your specific role, the technical decisions and trade-offs you made, obstacles overcome, and the measurable outcome. Emphasize YOUR contribution and what you learned.

questions.qText: Tell me about a disagreement with a teammate or manager.

blocks.text: Show you attack ideas, not people: listen first, restate their concern, bring data/a prototype instead of opinion, agree on a decision + criteria, and keep the relationship intact. End with the outcome and what you'd do again.

questions.qText: Manager round: how do you handle ambiguity and prioritize under pressure?

blocks.text: Clarify the goal and constraints, break work into the smallest valuable slice, identify the riskiest assumption and de-risk it first, communicate trade-offs and timelines early, and prioritize by impact vs effort. Give a concrete example where you shipped despite unclear requirements.

questions.qText: Where do you see yourself in 5 years / why this company?

blocks.text: Growth: strong engineer now → senior owning design and mentoring later. For 'why this company', do 15 minutes of research and connect ONE real thing (product, scale, engineering culture) to your experience. Have 3 thoughtful questions ready for them.
